cards.note: dual of סוֹגֵר "closer" — the marks that shut a phrase in
single mark — makes foreign sounds (ג׳=j, צ׳=ch) and abbreviations
dual of geresh — acronyms (תנ״ך) and Hebrew numerals; sits before the last letter
quotes — set off speech and titles
root ס־ג־ר; also 'turn off' a device, 'close' a deal
חנות סגורה = closed shop; biblical זהב סגור = pure gold
what encloses — picture frame → abstract framework
מאמר מוסגר = an aside 'shut inside' the parens

etymology.p: The Semitic root for shut / seal-off. Hebrew sagar spawns a whole family: סוגריים (parentheses = "the closers"), מסגרת (frame), מוסגר (parenthetical/enclosed), סגר (lockdown). The "dam / block" image is actually a sibling root, ס־כ־ר (Akkadian sekēru; Hebrew סכר "dam"). Aramaic סגר is solid — in Daniel, God "shut" (sagar) the lions' mouths. Ugaritic sgr, though, is a different word — a noun "watchman / guard" (per Modern South Arabian), not the "close" verb.

geresh and gershayim are named from ג־ר־ש "expel / drive out" (גירש "expelled", גירושין "divorce"). They began as names of a Masoretic cantillation accent (the geresh ta'am) and were later borrowed for the typographic marks: ׳ for foreign sounds & abbreviations, ״ for acronyms & numerals.

Hebrew names several marks as pairs, with the ־ַיִם dual ending: סוֹגְרַיִם (parens), גֵּרְשַׁיִם (gershayim), נְקֻדָּתַיִם (colon = "two dots") — the same logic as נעליים (shoes) and אוזניים (ears), things that come in twos.

English & Spanish parenthesis = Greek para-en-thesis, "placed in beside" — it names the inserted aside. Hebrew סוגריים = "the closers" — it names the marks that enclose. Greek points at the content; Hebrew at the brackets.
